nativeRange.habitat: Peat swamp forests, blackwater streams, and slow-moving forest rivers with highly acidic, tannin-stained water over leaf litter substrate.

diet.notes: The Chocolate Gourami is a micropredator that primarily accepts live and frozen micro-foods: daphnia, baby brine shrimp (Artemia nauplii), blackworms, grindal worms, and mosquito larvae. Many specimens initially refuse prepared or pellet foods; some eventually adapt to high-quality micropellets with patient conditioning. Feeding with live food is strongly advised, especially when first acquired.

goodWith: small corydoras in same pH range
avoidWith: fish requiring harder or neutral water

imageCandidates.notes: Brian Gratwicke, 3000x1855. Tack-sharp full-profile view; classic chocolate-brown body with pale cream vertical bars, pointed snout and orange-edged fins clearly shown on a vibrant live specimen.
Dennis White, 2048x1364. Sharp, well-lit individual in a natural blackwater-style setting; bold cream vertical bars and chocolate coloration are strongly diagnostic.
OSt DD, 600x397. Different reddish-brown individual among plants; adds variety, though lower resolution and fainter barring than the recommended pick.

summary: The Chocolate Gourami (Sphaerichthys osphromenoides) is a small blackwater specialist native to the peat swamp forests and acidic streams of Borneo and Sumatra. Widely considered one of the more demanding freshwater gouramis, it requires extremely soft, acidic water and is notably susceptible to bacterial disease. It is also unusual within its family as a maternal mouthbrooder.
careNotes: STRICT blackwater specialist — not a typical gourami and should not be treated as one. The species requires RO/DI water remineralized to near-zero hardness, supplemented with Indian Almond leaves, peat extract, or peat filtration to achieve a pH of 4.0–6.5 and a gH under 5. Tap water with any hardness is generally unsuitable without conditioning. The Chocolate Gourami tends to be highly susceptible to bacterial infections, with Mycobacterium (fish tuberculosis) reported frequently in the hobby; pristine water quality and stress-free conditions are essential. The species maintains a social hierarchy within groups; keeping a trio (1 male to 2 females) or a larger group of six or more is generally advised to distribute social tension. The tank should be dimly lit with a tightly-fitting lid — fry require access to warm, humid air above the water surface to properly develop their labyrinth organ (the specialized breathing structure that allows gouramis to breathe atmospheric air directly). The species will often refuse prepared foods initially; live or frozen micro-foods should be offered exclusively until the fish has settled into its new environment.
breedingNotes: Maternal mouthbrooder — the female orally incubates eggs for approximately 7–20 days, releasing 10–40 fully-formed juveniles. Conditioning with live foods is generally needed to trigger spawning. Soft, acidic, warm water (pH 4.5–5.5, 27–29°C) with minimal flow and heavy planting tends to encourage breeding. The female should not be disturbed during the brooding period. Fry are relatively large at release and can typically accept newly hatched brine shrimp or microworms immediately. A tightly sealed tank cover is important so that humid air above the water surface supports proper labyrinth organ development in fry.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish
primary.notes: Primary husbandry source; Aquarium Co-Op returned 404 so SeriouslyFish used as primary.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (6.0 cm TL), temperature range (24–27°C), pH (4.0–6.0), gH (0–4 dH), native range (Indonesia and Malaysia). FishBase notes 'males brooding' but this is a known documentation discrepancy — multiple specialist sources confirm the species is a maternal mouthbrooder.

additional.name: Aquadiction
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (6 cm), temperature (25–28°C), pH (4.0–6.0), gH (2–10), lifespan (5–8 years), tank minimum (20 gal / ~76L). Confirms maternal mouthbrooder, grouping of 6+.

fish.breedingNotes: The Chocolate Gourami is a maternal mouthbrooder: the female incubates 10–40 eggs orally for 7–20 days, releasing fully-formed juveniles when brooding is complete. Spawning typically requires stable, soft, acidic water and conditioning with live foods.
